title: The Imitation Game: Defending against AI's Dark Side!
gist: Damodaran dissects an AI-generated deepfake video and Instagram post impersonating him to solicit investment in a fake Palantir-linked club promising 60%+ returns, using the episode to build a general framework for spotting AI investment scams and to explain why his own radically open-access publishing philosophy makes him an easy target.

themes.theme: AI deepfake investment scam targeting Damodaran

insights.claim: An AI-generated Instagram post and a deepfake video impersonating Aswath Damodaran surfaced in mid-2025, using his likeness and voice to solicit investors into a club with promises of super-normal returns.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Investors should treat any social-media video or post 'from' a well-known market commentator soliciting direct investment as a high-probability scam, regardless of how authentic it looks or sounds.
insights.quote: there is now a video that looks and sounds like me, adding to the sales pitch with promises of super-normal returns if they reach out, and presumably send their money in.
insights.locator: A few weeks ago, I started receiving a stream of message

insights.claim: The fake video was built from thousands of hours of Damodaran's own publicly posted YouTube lecture videos, which gave the AI model enough raw material to convincingly replicate his look and voice.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: The more public video/audio content a person or company executive has online, the easier and cheaper it becomes for scammers to generate a convincing deepfake of them for fraud.
insights.quote: The video looks and sounds like me, which should be no surprise since it had thousands of hours of YouTube videos to use as raw data.

insights.claim: Damodaran predicts AI-driven impersonation scams targeting investing personalities, political figures and experts will keep improving in quality and become far more prevalent going forward.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Expect a rising volume of increasingly convincing deepfake-driven investment scams; authenticity heuristics based on appearance/voice will keep degrading as a defense.
insights.quote: The worse news is that AI technology is evolving, and it will get better on every one of these fronts at imitating others, and you should prepare yourself for a deluge of investment scams.
insights.locator: In sum, there is good news and bad news

insights.claim: A more sophisticated 'Damodaran Bot' already exists in development at NYU Stern with sanctioned access to his entire body of work, and Damodaran believes an AI trained this thoroughly could produce scam content far harder to detect than the current crude fakes.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: The gap between today's easily-spotted AI scams and near-future ones trained on a full corpus of a person's work could close quickly, so detection frameworks need to rely on behavior/content, not surface polish.
insights.quote: I will wager that the Damodaran Bot that I mentioned earlier on in this post that is being developed at NYU Stern would have created a post that would have been much more difficult for you to detect as fake, making it a Frankenstein monster perhaps in the making.
insights.locator: In sum, there is good news and bad news

themes.theme: Grading the scam: what the AI got right vs wrong

insights.claim: The scam's authenticity signals were strong on style and stock selection: it wrote in Damodaran's characteristic voice and referenced Nvidia and Palantir, two companies he has actually owned or publicly analyzed, lending it credibility.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Scammers increasingly anchor fake endorsements to real, verifiable facts about a target's known positions to make fabricated claims more believable — investors must verify claims independently, not just check plausibility.
insights.quote: it also picked Nvidia and Palantir as the two firms to highlight, the first a company that I own and have valued on my blog, and the second a company that I have been talking about as one that I am interested in owning, at the right price, giving it a patina of authenticity.

insights.claim: The scam described Palantir as 'overhyped', a word Damodaran says he rarely uses, and offered no substantive valuation analysis behind the claim, exposing it as a fabrication to anyone familiar with his actual writing style and rigor.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: A reliable tell for AI-generated financial content impersonating an analyst is vague, unsubstantiated claims where the real person would normally show detailed valuation work.
insights.quote: The scam does mention that Palantir is "overhyped", a word that I use rarely, and while it talks about the company's valuation, it is cagey about what that value is and there is little of substance to back up the claim.

insights.claim: Damodaran's actual framework for valuing Palantir requires splitting it into two distinct businesses: a government segment (lower-margin, stickier, lower-growth) and a commercial segment (higher-margin, more volatile, higher-growth), rather than extrapolating a single blended revenue-growth trend.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Investors valuing Palantir should model government and commercial revenue streams separately with different margin and growth assumptions rather than applying one blended multiple.
insights.quote: you need a story of a data/software firm, with two channels for value creation, one of which looks at the government as a customer (a lower-margin, stickier and lower growth business) and the other at its commercial market (higher margin, more volatile and higher growth).

insights.claim: The scam's most decisive tells were content-based, not stylistic: it pushed people to join an exclusive investment club and promised guaranteed returns of 60% or higher, both of which directly contradict Damodaran's decades-long, publicly documented refusal to give stock advice or promise market-beating returns.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Understanding a public figure's stated investment philosophy and behavioral history gives investors a reliable template for spotting impersonation scams that no amount of visual/audio fidelity can fake.
insights.quote: there is a part of this scam where I purportedly promise investors who decide to partake that they will generate returns of 60% or higher, and as someone who has chronicled that not only do most active investors not keep up with the market, and argued that anyone who promises to deliver substantially more than the market in the long term is either a liar or fraud, this is clearly not me.

themes.theme: Framework for detecting and defending against AI investment scams

insights.claim: Visual and audio authenticity ('looks and sounds like') is no longer a valid test for verifying whether investment content genuinely comes from the person it claims to, given current AI video/voice cloning capability.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Investors must shift verification from sensory cues (does it look/sound right) to content and behavioral cues (does the message match the person's documented philosophy and track record of public statements).
insights.quote: I hope that you have concluded that “looks and sounds Iike” is no longer good enough to meet the authenticity test.

insights.claim: Damodaran argues that 'smart money' able to consistently beat the market over long periods largely does not exist — outperformance by hedge funds and private equity is more often luck than skill, and it tends to be fleeting.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Any pitch implying an investor or club can reliably deliver consistent market-beating returns should be treated as a red flag rather than a credential.
insights.quote: when you see a group of investors (hedge funds, private equity) beating the market, luck is more of a contributor as skill, and success is fleeting.

insights.claim: Screening investment pitches for outlandish return claims and arrogance is an effective filter because most scammers, lacking sophistication, default to bombastic promises rather than humble, nuanced claims.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: A simple, low-effort screen — reject any pitch promising outsized guaranteed returns or expressing overconfidence — eliminates the majority of scam attempts.
insights.quote: Since most scammers don't understand the subtlety of this idea, screening investment sales pitches for outlandish claims alone will eliminate most scams.

insights.claim: Before investing with anyone based on a pitch or virtual meeting, investors should evaluate that person's underlying investment philosophy for consistency and coherence, not just their claimed track record — this also creates a template for detecting impersonators.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Due diligence should focus on whether the pitched philosophy is internally consistent with the person's known body of work, not on returns claims alone.
insights.quote: It is malpractice to invest with anyone, no matter what their reputation for earning high returns, without understanding that person's investment philosophy, and this understanding will also give you a template for spotting fakes using that person's name.

insights.claim: ROMO (regret over missing out) and FOMO (fear of missing out) are the two emotional triggers — jealousy/conspiracy-thinking and greed, respectively — that scammers exploit, and Damodaran frames scams as requiring active emotional participation from the victim, not just deception by the scammer.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Investors should recognize when a pitch is deliberately triggering fear of missing a multi-bagger or regret over past missed winners (e.g., Facebook IPO, early bitcoin) as a psychological manipulation tactic, and treat that as a warning sign rather than a call to act.
insights.quote: scams are more akin to tangos, where each side needs the other. The scammer's techniques work because they trigger the emotions (fear, greed) of the scammed, to respond, and AI will only make this easier to do.

insights.claim: Damodaran concludes that regulators and government offer little real protection against AI-driven investment scams, and that individual investor vigilance ('caveat emptor') is the only practical defense.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Investors should not assume regulatory bodies will keep pace with AI-driven fraud and must build their own personal verification discipline rather than relying on external safeguards.
insights.quote: Looking to regulators or the government to protection will do little more than offer false comfort, and the best defense is “caveat emptor” or “buyer beware”.

themes.theme: Damodaran's open-access publishing philosophy as both strength and vulnerability

insights.claim: Damodaran publishes essentially his entire professional output — class videos, data, spreadsheets, papers, and blog posts — free and without paywalls across his webpage, YouTube, Substack, LinkedIn, Google Blogger and X, which he acknowledges makes his full body of work fully accessible to any AI system for training.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Public figures and firms with large, unprotected public content libraries should recognize they are prime raw material for AI impersonation and plan reputational defenses accordingly.
insights.quote: I cannot think of a single item of content that I have produced in the last decade that is not on one of these platforms, making my professional life an open book, and thus also accessible to any AI entity.

insights.claim: Damodaran maintains a strict personal policy of never giving stock picks or investment advice, valuing stocks and acting on those valuations for himself only, which he treats as core to his credibility and now as a key marker for identifying fakes using his name.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Any content purporting to be from Damodaran (or similar independent analysts with a stated no-advice policy) that directs people to buy/sell specific positions should be treated as inauthentic by default.
insights.quote: it is neither my place nor my role to try to get other people to do the same. ... The essence of good investing is taking ownership of your investment actions, and it is antithetical to that view of the world for me or anyone else to be telling you what to buy or sell.
insights.locator: No stock picks or investment advice

insights.claim: Damodaran avoids all commercial entanglements (no advisory, consulting or director roles with banks, consultants or investment firms) specifically to preserve a perception of being unbiased, which he believes is central to why people trust his content.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: For investors evaluating any market commentator's credibility, an absence of commercial ties/monetization is a signal worth weighting, and its presence in a scam pitch (e.g., paid clubs) is itself a red flag.
insights.quote: I fear that formalizing a link with any commercial entity (bank, consultant, investor), whether as advisor, consultant or as director, opens the door to the perception of bias.

insights.claim: Damodaran has faced escalating tiers of unauthorized use of his name and content over the years — from benign borrowing, to plagiarism, to outright scams selling his data/videos or soliciting investment — and views AI as the latest and most dangerous escalation of a long-running problem, not a novel one.
insights.speaker: Aswath Damodaran
insights.so_what: Reputational-impersonation risk for public financial commentators is a spectrum that AI accelerates rather than creates, implying defenses need to address the full range from plagiarism to fraud, not just deepfakes.
insights.quote: The most malignant variations of this game are scams, where the scammers use my content or name to separate people from their money - the education companies that used my YouTube videos and charge for classes, the data sites that copy my data or spreadsheets and sell them to people, and the valuation/investment sites that try to get people to invest money, with my name as a draw.
